mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz E300 / E450 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E300 / E450 engine typically costs £4,000–£7,500 supply only, with the M256 and M254 variants at the higher end. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500 depending on the specialist. A full engine replacement on a Mercedes can range from £9,000 to £13,000. Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200 for cleaning and testing, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your E-Class.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz E300 / E450 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine your E-Class has. The pre-facelift E450 with the M276 V6 is widely considered one of Mercedes' most reliable engines. The pre-facelift E300 with the M274 is a capable engine but has timing chain vulnerabilities. The facelift E450 with the M256 straight-six is a smooth and powerful engine, but owners must be vigilant about the coolant leak recall and timing chain wear. The facelift E300 with the M254 has reported cylinder head failures. With proper maintenance and proactive attention to known issues, all can be reliable — but the M276 is the standout for reliability.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz E300 / E450?

acceptedAnswer.text: Pre-facelift E300 (M274): Timing chain stretch, camshaft actuator issues. Pre-facelift E450 (M276): Oil cooler seal leaks; otherwise considered very reliable. Facelift E300 (M254): Cylinder head failures reported, 48V system faults. Facelift E450 (M256): Coolant leak recall, timing chain wear, 48V electrical system faults, NANOSLIDE bore scoring concerns.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an E300 / E450 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the vehicle value and engine era. Against a well-kept E450 worth £25,000–£40,000, a £6,000–£8,000 recon is 15–25% of the car's value — worth it. Against a tired pre-facelift example with significant body issues, the same recon may approach our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold — get a scrap valuation first. The M276-engined pre-facelift E450 is the most desirable for reliability-focused buyers.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz E300 / E450 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Pre-facelift E300 (2017–2020): M274. Pre-facelift E450 (2017–2020): M276. Facelift E300 (2020–present): M254. Facelift E450 (2020–present): M256. Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the engine block.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M256 coolant leak, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The coolant leak is covered under a Mercedes-Benz recall for affected vehicles. Check your VIN on the Mercedes-Benz UK recall portal — repairs are free at authorised dealers. If the leak has already caused severe engine damage (warped cylinder heads, electrical damage), engine replacement may be required. At the first sign of sweet smells, coolant loss or overheating, stop driving immediately.

eraMap.title: The E300 / E450 THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: 2.0L turbo four — 241 bhp, 273 lb-ft; timing chain stretch and camshaft actuator issues are the main concerns
3.0L twin-turbo V6 — 362 bhp, 354 lb-ft; considered one of MB's most reliable motors; oil cooler seals are the primary watch item
2.0L turbo four — 255 bhp, 295 lb-ft + 48V mild hybrid; newer design; cylinder head failures reported on some units
3.0L turbo six — 362–367 bhp, 500 Nm + 48V EQ Boost; smooth and powerful; coolant leaks and timing chain wear are the critical watch items

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: E-Class E300 / E450 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance — catch it before catastrophic failure
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Check VIN with Mercedes — free repair at dealer

rows.problem: M256 coolant leak (out of recall — severe)
rows.whenItMakesSense: Only if caught early; often more cost-effective to replace engine
Viable repair — catch it before it causes further issues
Labour-intensive but essential — prevent oil loss

buyingChecks.title: Buying This E300 / E450? Check These First
buyIf: * Facelift E450 with M256 recall work completed (check VIN with Mercedes)
* Pre-facelift E450 with the M276 V6 (considered one of MB's most reliable engines)
* Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (critical for timing chain longevity)
* No coolant loss or sweet smells from the engine bay (M256 coolant leak warning)
* Cabrio/coupe models with roof cords replaced (2018–2019 models prone to failure — £6,000 repair out of warranty)
avoidIf: * Facelift E450 with no recall history — coolant leak may be imminent
* Cold-start rattling and no timing chain history — assume it's overdue
* No service history — all engines are sensitive to oil quality
* Unexplained coolant loss or sweet smells — M256 coolant leak may be developing
* 48V warning lights — electrical system may be compromised
* Cabrio models with roof cord issues and no warranty — £6,000 repair out of pocket

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The modern E-Class E300 and E450 represent Mercedes-Benz's premium petrol offerings in the W213 generation — combining luxury, performance and advanced technology. The pre-facelift E450 with the M276 V6 is widely considered one of Mercedes' most reliable engines, making it a strong used buy. The facelift E450 with the M256 straight-six is a "really nice" engine, but owners must be vigilant about the coolant leak recall and timing chain wear. The E300 (both M274 and M254) offers strong performance from a 2.0-litre four-cylinder, but the M254 has reported cylinder head failures. With typical E-Class values ranging from £20,000 (pre-facelift) to £50,000+ (facelift), an engine replacement is a major financial commitment — a well-maintained pre-facelift E450 M276 with full service history is the safest choice for reliability-focused buyers.

cta.label: → Compare E300 / E450 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz E-Class E300 / E450 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Recall Status — Check VIN on Mercedes-Benz UK recall portal for M256 coolant leak recall (facelift E450) — see the map above
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering (timing chain warning — all models)
* Coolant System — Check for sweet smells, white residue, unexplained coolant loss (M256 coolant leak warning)
* Service History — Including which engine era (M274, M276, M254 or M256) and oil-change discipline
* 48V System — Check for warning lights; verify stop-start functions correctly (facelift models)
* Oil Leaks — Check around the V of the engine for oil cooler seal leaks (M276 — pre-facelift E450)
* Cabrio Roof — On cabrio models, check roof operation and cords; 2018–2019 models prone to failure
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, cooling, 48V or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
